item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I was going up the stair
I met a man who wasn’t there
He wasn’t there again today
Oh how I wish he’d go away

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought for some days about the question of making it accessible to the reader, and I have two suggestions that could work for you, either for this, or future articles.

First, you could make two or three versions of the article with distinct clearance levels, so the low level version is the main article, while the high level is the explanation. This model was used on a bunch of good skips. SCP-2317 comes to mind, though it isn't the best example for this article.

Second, you could rely on giving spoilers on the comments. It is a cheap trick for this, but sometimes it is worth it. Though some readers won't even look here, several others are already used to look into the comments to see if they find something about the skip they couldn't see by themselves.

Anyway, my personal opinion stands the same, but it isn't at all a bad execution, and I don't think you have to rewrite this unless you want to. I look forward to read new articles from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fair enough.

Although, unless I get significantly more responses along those lines, I think I'll leave it as it is, simply because… well… when I first wrote it, the complaints I got all pertained to it being too vague for the actual reader! I deliberately made it more obvious simply because no one seemed to understand it (and as is evident in some of the comments on it, some people still don't really understand my intended meaning!).

So in a way, yes, I agree, it is too obvious what's going on, but to allow others to enjoy it, I had to be less vague to place it in a state that the majority of readers could understand. So I'm sorry about that!

Also, on the topic of Tales, I'm even worse at writing those in a vague but comprehensible manner, so I think someone else would have to do that.

Anyway, thanks for your feedback, I really appreciate it! I'll see if I can tone down the obviousness a tad more on future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is certainly well written, that's for sure. It is a creative effect with a deep and well developed story behind, and the delivery was fairly well done.

+ But I have two major issues that lead ultimately to a downvote.
– hide block

First, this is definitely not my cup of tea about the Foundation, and I'm not sure if this kind of project belongs to the mainlist. For obvious reasons I can't be sure about my judgment on this, but I think Project EXPUNGED would be better placed on a tale.

Second, it is too obvious, and this is a fairly grave mistake on this article. As interesting as the story is, the document is written in such a way as to create a huge plot hole. Why is the maintenance document of a higher level than the Observation one? Why such limited timing for research, without trying for alternative ways of observation? As much as a scientist of the Foundation knows that weird stuff exists, wouldn't the reference to a virulent kind of radiation bring more attention than avoid it?

The phenomenon itself makes some of the truth relatively easy to guess. Even without the previous holes, an average researcher could easily conclude that the D-Class leader exists on some kind of parallel reality or time, and its continued actions are affecting the main reality. And it makes it fairly obvious, again, that there is something going on on b12. The interview just makes everything crystal clear, specially with both being neutralized as witnesses at the end, and it should at least be a higher clearance document.

You don't need to keep the letter in the end O5 clearance, though. Since the staff of the actual Project is level 4, the letter could stay at level 4 as a piece of research data.


Maybe you could break this story in a skip and a tale, and clean the skip. Maybe you could add some twist, like the Ethics Committee finding out and unveiling the project, and the current document being really more revealing. But for now, I'll downvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually the explanation for the illegible parts is in the very note. This improvised reality anchor wasn't perfect, and some data disappeared.

It is up to you if this explanation is satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. I love these kinds of "metaphysical" SCPs, and you did an entertaining and surprisingly thorough job of explaining the quirks of this one.

My one gripe is the formatting of the final note. The handwritten font with [ILLEGIBLE]s mixed in is jarring for me. Either the note would be handwritten, in which case we'd just see the note and see for ourselves why it's illegible, or it would be transcript of the note, in which case there would be no need for such a font.

A minor issue. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though the title gives the essence of this away, I still enjoyed it immensely. +1, and I'd really love to see this expanded upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you continue writing like this, that will never happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much, I'm glad you liked it! Now I just hope I don't end up with a Second SCP Curse instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic, especially for a first article. This is a good example of the fact that there is no "First SCP Curse".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I've been very anxious that people would shoot me down due to this being my first article, so your kind words mean a lot to me. I shall try to create even better things in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! A tale about Project EXPUNGED would be awesome. I deliberately tried to leave the nature of it somewhat vague so people could formulate their own theories in tales or just their own head canons. It'd be cool to see someone take advantage of that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a very good read - I had some minor squalms about the assessed danger level, but after reading further I felt you addressed how they came to their current judgement. Also, the level of technical skill you show is impressive - I'm looking to future SCPs from you.

Solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this, this was good

The idea of a D-Class and a Rogue Researcher teaming up sounds bad ass. And I like the idea of a Final Destination like death to each person individually at different times thing.

I am really tempted to turn Project EXPUNGE into a Tale or a series or something like that because the idea amuses me

You get all my +1's, my man
